title: Provider
objectives: Understand Provider via a real-life analogy
Use Consumer, Selector, context.read, context.watch correctly
Recognize why Riverpod is the modern successor

sections.markdown:
**Think of Provider as a public bulletin board mounted at the top of a school hallway.**

- Anyone walking underneath (any child widget in the tree) can look up and read the current announcement ÔÇö a user profile, a cart, a theme.
- When someone updates the board (`notifyListeners()`), everyone who was WATCHING the board (via `context.watch<T>()` or a `Consumer<T>`) refreshes their notes automatically.
- Anyone who only wants to check it ONCE without joining the watchlist (`context.read<T>()`) can ÔÇö but they won't be notified of future changes.
- The board itself is put up ONCE near the top of the tree (`ChangeNotifierProvider(create: () => Model())`), and everyone downstream can find it via `context.of<T>()`.

**Why this matters:** Provider was Flutter's dominant state library from 2019ÔÇô2022, and it's still very common ÔÇö every legacy codebase you inherit will use it. It also teaches the InheritedWidget mental model, which underpins Riverpod, `Theme.of`, `MediaQuery.of`, and every other 'walk-the-tree' API in Flutter. Even if you'll write new apps in Riverpod, understanding Provider is a required part of the toolkit.

**Provider** is a state-management package built on top of Flutter's `InheritedWidget` mechanism. You wrap part of the tree in a `Provider` (or a variant like `ChangeNotifierProvider`, `StreamProvider`, `FutureProvider`) and any descendant can read it via `Provider.of<T>(context)`, `context.watch<T>()`, or a `Consumer<T>` widget. `notifyListeners()` on a `ChangeNotifier` rebuilds every subscriber.
## The Provider family ÔÇö which one to use when

rows: Mutable model that calls `notifyListeners()` on change
Compute T from another provider (e.g., ApiClient from Auth)

sections.markdown: ## Canonical pattern ÔÇö ChangeNotifierProvider + Consumer
## Selector ÔÇö narrow the rebuild scope
## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: `context.watch<T>()` in a callback.** Only use `watch` in `build()`. In `onPressed` etc., use `context.read<T>()` ÔÇö you want the current value, not a subscription.

**Mistake 2: Forgetting `notifyListeners()` after a mutation.** State changes but the UI never rebuilds ÔÇö no visible bug for the developer, silent for the user. Trigger it every time the outside-observable state changes.
**Mistake 3: Notifying while widgets are building.** Calling `notifyListeners()` inside a `build()` method throws in debug. Do state changes in callbacks, not in `build`.

**Mistake 4: Wrapping the whole widget tree in `Consumer<T>`.** Every notification rebuilds it. Use `Selector<T, X>` to narrow, or move the `Consumer` down closer to the widgets that actually depend on the state.

**Mistake 5: Not disposing.** A `ChangeNotifierProvider` disposes its notifier automatically. But if you manage the notifier yourself (`Provider.value`), you must call `dispose()` ÔÇö otherwise listeners leak.
## Best practices
**1. `context.watch<T>()` in `build`, `context.read<T>()` in callbacks.** The rule that fixes most Provider bugs.
**2. Prefer `Selector<T, X>` for narrow subscriptions.** Rebuilds only when the extracted slice changes.
**3. Provide as HIGH as needed, no higher.** If only two screens need CartModel, provide near their common ancestor ÔÇö not at `MaterialApp` root. Fewer rebuilds.
**4. Use `ProxyProvider` when one provider derives from another.** Cleaner than manual wiring.
**5. For new projects in 2026, prefer Riverpod.** Same conceptual model, better ergonomics (no `BuildContext`, compile-safe DI, `AsyncValue`, easier testing).
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) ÔÇö Counter with ChangeNotifierProvider.** `CounterModel` with an `_count` field; increment button.
**Practice 2 (Medium) ÔÇö Cart across screens.** Home + Cart tabs share a `CartModel` via a single provider at the app root.
**Practice 3 (Medium) ÔÇö Selector for count-only rebuild.** Watch the whole model somewhere; use `Selector` to isolate a `count` widget; observe fewer rebuilds via DevTools' Highlight Rebuilds.
**Practice 4 (Hard) ÔÇö MultiProvider.** Wrap the app with multiple providers (Auth, Theme, Cart). Access each independently.
**Practice 5 (Expert) ÔÇö Migrate to Riverpod.** Convert a Provider-based screen to Riverpod (`NotifierProvider`); compare LOC + boilerplate.
## Interview Q&A ÔÇö with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Provider and what problem does it solve?**

**A:** Provider is a state-management package built on top of Flutter's `InheritedWidget` mechanism. It solves the 'how do I share state across many widgets without passing callbacks 10 levels deep' problem. You wrap part of the tree in a Provider; any descendant can read that state via `Provider.of<T>(context)`, `context.watch<T>()`, or `Consumer<T>`. Combined with a `ChangeNotifier` model, subscribers auto-rebuild when the model calls `notifyListeners()`. It was Flutter's dominant state solution 2019-2022; Riverpod (its spiritual successor) is now the modern default, but Provider is still widespread in existing code and worth understanding fluently.

**Q2: `context.watch<T>()` vs `context.read<T>()` vs `Consumer<T>`?**

**A:** **`context.watch<T>()`** ÔÇö reads the value AND subscribes; the calling widget rebuilds on every `notifyListeners()`. Use inside `build()`. **`context.read<T>()`** ÔÇö reads the current value WITHOUT subscribing; the calling widget does NOT rebuild. Use inside callbacks (`onPressed`) where you just want to call a method or read the current state once. **`Consumer<T>(builder: ...)`** ÔÇö same as `watch` but scopes the rebuild to the builder's subtree; the enclosing `build()` isn't invalidated. Rule: `watch` in build, `read` in callbacks, `Consumer` when you want to isolate the rebuild region.

**Q3: What is `Selector<T, X>` and when do you use it?**

**A:** `Selector<T, X>` is like `Consumer<T>` but you extract only a SLICE of `T` via a `selector: (context, model) => model.field` callback. The builder rebuilds only when the extracted value changes. Without a Selector, `Consumer<CartModel>` rebuilds on ANY `notifyListeners()` ÔÇö even if you only care about `count` and the notification was about `sortOrder`. With `Selector<CartModel, int>`, it rebuilds only when `count` changes. This is the exact equivalent of Riverpod's `ref.watch(p.select((s) => s.field))`. Essential for keeping rebuilds surgical in complex apps.

**Q4: How does `Provider` differ from `ChangeNotifierProvider`?**

**A:** **`Provider<T>`** ÔÇö provides a value that NEVER notifies. Great for dependency injection: repositories, services, immutable config objects. Descendants that `read` it will not rebuild. **`ChangeNotifierProvider<T>`** ÔÇö provides a `ChangeNotifier` subclass. `notifyListeners()` triggers rebuilds of all subscribers. Great for mutable state. There are also `StreamProvider<T>` (wraps a Stream), `FutureProvider<T>` (wraps a Future), `ProxyProvider<A, T>` (T derives from A), and value variants (`Provider.value`) for pre-existing instances. Pick based on the shape of your state: pure DI Ôćĺ `Provider`, mutable model Ôćĺ `ChangeNotifierProvider`, async Ôćĺ `Stream`/`FutureProvider`.

**Q5: What's `MultiProvider` and when do you need it?**

**A:** `MultiProvider(providers: [P1, P2, P3])` composes multiple providers into one widget instead of nesting them (`Provider(child: Provider(child: Provider(child: ...)))`). Pure sugar ÔÇö the behavior is identical to nesting, but the widget tree is flatter and easier to read. Use it whenever your app has multiple providers at the same scope level: `MultiProvider(providers: [ChangeNotifierProvider(create: (_) => Auth()), ChangeNotifierProvider(create: (_) => Theme()), Provider(create: (_) => Api()) ], child: MyApp())`. It's the standard pattern for wiring up a project's top-level state.

**Q6: Why is Riverpod considered Provider's successor?**

**A:** Riverpod (from Provider's author) fixes Provider's structural issues: **(1) No `BuildContext` required** ÔÇö providers are top-level `final` variables read via a `ref` object; you can access state outside of widgets (tests, controllers, isolates). **(2) Compile-safe** ÔÇö if a provider isn't reachable, the error is at build time, not `ProviderNotFoundException` at runtime. **(3) `AsyncValue<T>`** ÔÇö sealed loading/data/error state built-in; kills ad-hoc `bool _loading; String? _error` patterns. **(4) `autoDispose`** ÔÇö automatic cleanup when no widget watches. **(5) Better testing** ÔÇö `ProviderContainer` runs state without pumping widgets. Same conceptual model (an InheritedWidget-style dependency graph), better ergonomics. For NEW projects in 2026 Ôćĺ Riverpod. Existing Provider code is fine to keep.

**Q7: What causes `ProviderNotFoundException`?**

**A:** `Provider.of<T>(context)` was called from a widget that isn't a descendant of the `Provider<T>` that provides `T`. Common causes: **(1) Provider not at the right level** ÔÇö you provided `CartModel` inside a screen, but a different screen tries to read it. Move the provider up. **(2) Called from a builder that runs outside the provider scope** ÔÇö e.g., a dialog opened via `showDialog` runs in the ROOT navigator's overlay, above your provider. Fix: use the `builder:` field of `showDialog` that inherits the current context, or pass the value explicitly. **(3) Wrong type** ÔÇö `Provider<Cart>` but you asked for `Provider<CartModel>`. Fix: match the types exactly. This is Provider's most common error; Riverpod moves it to compile time.

## Real-world usage

- **Existing Flutter apps 2019-2022** ÔÇö the dominant pattern; still very common.
- **`FirebaseAuth.instance.authStateChanges()`** naturally maps to `StreamProvider<User?>`.
- **Feature flags / A/B configs** ÔÇö `Provider<FlagsService>` for DI.
- **Design system tokens** ÔÇö `ChangeNotifierProvider<AppTheme>` for hot theme changes.
- **Small-to-medium apps** ÔÇö Provider is still simple and sufficient.
- **Tests** ÔÇö `ChangeNotifierProvider.value(value: fakeCart, child: MyScreen)`.

## Advanced notes

- **`InheritedNotifier` under the hood** ÔÇö Provider uses this to propagate.
- **`ProxyProvider<A, T>` / `ProxyProvider2` / ÔÇŽ** ÔÇö derive one provider from others (e.g., `ApiClient` from `AuthToken`).
- **`Provider.value` vs `Provider(create: ...)`** ÔÇö value skips lifecycle management (no dispose); use only if you own the object's lifecycle elsewhere.
- **`ListenableProvider`** ÔÇö provide any `Listenable` (`ValueNotifier`, `AnimationController`).
- **`context.select<T, R>((T v) => v.field)`** ÔÇö inline version of `Selector` without a wrapping widget.
- **`Consumer2`, `Consumer3`** ÔÇö subscribe to multiple providers in one builder.
- **Testing** ÔÇö `MaterialApp` wrapped in a `MultiProvider` fed with mocks.
- **Migration to Riverpod** ÔÇö usually 1:1 mapping; `ChangeNotifierProvider` Ôćĺ `NotifierProvider`; `StreamProvider` Ôćĺ `StreamProvider`; `ProxyProvider` Ôćĺ derived providers.
- **`provider.debugCheckInvalidValueType`** ÔÇö helper for validation.
- **`riverpod_generator`** ÔÇö code-gen for cleaner Riverpod syntax.

flashcards.back: InheritedWidget ÔÇö Flutter's built-in tree-walking dependency mechanism.
watch = read + subscribe (in build). read = read only (in callbacks).
Use Selector<T, X> or context.select((v) => v.field) to subscribe to a slice.
Composing multiple providers into a single, flat parent widget.
ProviderNotFoundException ÔÇö the calling widget isn't a descendant of the Provider.
Same idea. Riverpod: no BuildContext, compile-safe, AsyncValue, easier tests. New apps Ôćĺ Riverpod.

challenges.text: Selector<CartModel, int> to isolate a count widget.
StreamProvider<User?> from a Firebase authStateChanges stream.

Migrate one screen from Provider (ChangeNotifierProvider) to Riverpod (NotifierProvider); measure LOC delta.
